exclusion_rules.description: Palliative care is intended for patients with cancer, HIV/AIDS, neurological disorders, and other life-limiting or chronic progressive illnesses requiring pain relief and symptom control. This reflects clinical need, not an income or category bar — services are free and open to all who need them.

family_criteria.family_definition: Universal access based on clinical need — free palliative care is available to any patient with a life-limiting or chronic illness, and support extends to their family. No income test or formal enrollment is required.

covered_procedures: Free pain relief and symptom management for patients with life-limiting illness
Free access to essential narcotic drugs (oral morphine) for cancer pain
Palliative care for cancer, HIV/AIDS, and chronic neurological disorders
Psychosocial and emotional support for patients and families
Spiritual and bereavement support from diagnosis through end of life
Home-based palliative care visits where available
Palliative care at Community Health Centres and district hospitals
Palliative care wards / services at tertiary care facilities
Nursing care, wound care, and management of distressing symptoms
Counselling and caregiver training for family members
excluded_procedures: Curative or disease-modifying treatment (delivered under other programmes such as NPCDCS or PM-JAY)
Private hospice or private nursing home charges
Out-of-pocket purchase of supplies not stocked at the government facility

required_documents.name: Medical Records / Diagnosis
required_documents.notes: Existing diagnosis or referral helps the team plan care, but a patient in distress is assessed and supported regardless of paperwork.

required_documents.name: Any Government Photo ID
required_documents.notes: Helpful for hospital registration but not mandatory for receiving palliative care.

enrollment_channels: Government medical college / tertiary care facility
Regional Cancer Centre palliative care department
Home-based palliative care team (where available under NHM)
ASHA / ANM community health worker referral
enrollment_steps: Step 1: Ask your treating doctor, a CHC, or a district hospital about palliative care for pain relief and symptom control
Step 2: Get referred to the nearest government palliative care unit (district hospital, medical college, or regional cancer centre)
Step 3: The palliative care team assesses the patient's pain and symptoms free of cost
Step 4: Receive free pain-relief medicines, including oral morphine for cancer pain where indicated
Step 5: The team provides psychosocial, emotional, and spiritual support to the patient and family
Step 6: Where available, home-based visits and caregiver training are arranged
Step 7: Ongoing symptom management and bereavement support continue through the course of illness
description_for_embedding: The National Programme for Palliative Care (NPPC) is a central government programme under the Ministry of Health & Family Welfare, implemented through the National Health Mission, to make rational and quality pain relief and palliative care available and accessible to the needy as an integral part of healthcare at all levels. It provides FREE pain relief, symptom control, and psychosocial support to patients suffering from cancer, HIV/AIDS, chronic neurological disorders, and other life-limiting or chronic progressive illnesses, with no income test or means assessment — access is based on clinical need. Palliative care under NPPC can be delivered in tertiary care facilities, at Community Health Centres and district hospitals, and even in patients' homes through home-based care teams. The programme improves quality of life for patients and families facing serious illness by providing pain and symptom relief, emotional, psychosocial, spiritual, and bereavement support from diagnosis through the end of life. A key focus is ensuring availability of essential narcotic drugs, particularly oral morphine, for cancer pain; the Ministry has trained Registered Medical Practitioners to safely prescribe and dispense these medicines. The programme is integrated into the NHM Mission Flexipool, with States and UTs incorporating palliative care into their Programme Implementation Plans. Anyone caring for a terminally ill or chronically ill family member who needs pain relief, comfort care, or emotional support can seek free palliative care at a government district hospital, medical college, or regional cancer centre.
